California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornmission
June 28, 1991
Mr. Jin Marino
2340 Niles Street
Bakersfield, CA 93306
Re: Your Request for Informal
Ass i stance
our FiIe No. I-90-7 55
Dear !,tr. Marino:
This is in response to your questions regarding the conflict
or inieiest provisions of the Politicat nefori Act ithe "Act").1
since your lLtter does not cite a specific governmenta l.dec is ion,
we consider it to be a request for inforrnal assistance.z
OUESTIONS
1. 
As an enployee of an engineering company performing
services in connection with a particular project, are you
precluded fron participating in your capacity as a planning
tornrnissioner in decisions relating to property within 30o feet of
the project on which your company is working?
2. 
If your enployer is providing services to a project
subject to planning comrnission review, are you precluded from
participating in decisions relating to that proiect, if your
enployer has no ownership interest in the property?
3. 
Would the answers to the above questions change in the
event that you are avrarded a management position or options to buy
stock in the company that enploys you?
1 cor"a.r*"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. A]1 statutory references
are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indi,cated. Cornmission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regutations Section
18ooo, et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Di,vision 6 of the Californj,a Code of ReguLations.
2 Inforrnal assistance does not provide the requestor lrith the
inrnunity provided by an opinion or forrnal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 CaI. Code of Regs.
section 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J street, Suite 8oo . p.o. Box 8o7 o sacramento cA 958o4-o8o? . (916) 322.5660


Our Fi■e No.  I-90-755
Page 2
4. 
Is your personal participation in a project undertaken
by your employer a factor in deterrnining whether you have a
conflict of interest vis a vis a governnental decision pertaining
to that proj ect?
CONCLUS IONS
1. 
You may participate in governmental decisions relating
to a development adjacent to a project for which your empJ-oyer is
perforrning services if there would be no reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect, distinguishable fron the effect on the
pub).ic generatly, on your employer resulting from the decisions.
2. 
You nay not participate in governmental decisions
relating to a developnent for which your employer is providing
engineering services unless there lrouId be no financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect public AeneraIIy, on your enployer
resulting from those decisions.
3. 
If you obtain an ownership interest in your employing
company amounting to ten percent or nore, then the sources of
income to your enployer become sources of income to you, and that
could create a conflict of interest where one of those sources of
income was involved in a planning commission decision.
4. 
Even if you do not personally participate in a project
for which your enployer has contracted to provide engineering
services, you nay sti1l be required to disqualify yourself fron
participating in planning commission decisions relating to that
proj ect .
FACTS
You are a pJ.anning commissioner for the city of gakersfield.
You are also an employee of the Dewalt Corporation (r'Dei{alt'r), a
civil engineering and surveying firm. 
You are concerned about
participating in governmental decisions regarding projects where
Dewalt is (1) providing engineering services in connection with a
development adjacent to a project under review by the planning
commission and (2) providing services in connection with another
developnent which is under review by the planning connission.
You also anticipate that you nay be offered a nanagement
position at Dewalt. You would like to know if that anticipated
circurnstance would affect your participation in planning
connission decisions. you have inquired in your letter as to
whether ownership of enployee stock options would create a
conflict of interest for you in participating in decisions
affecting DeWaIt. However, during a subsequent telephone
conversation, you withdrew your question regarding stock options,
and therefore that question wirl not be addressed in this retter.


Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 3
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials 
from making,
participatinq in, or using their official 
position to influence
any governrnental decision in lrhich they know or have reasons to
know they have a financial interest. 
whether a public official
has a financial interest in a decision is governed by Section
87103, which provides, in part:
An official 
has a financial interest in a decision
within the neaning of section 87100 j.f it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a rnaterial financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public aenerally, on the officiaL
or a menber of his or her innediate family or on:
***
(c) Any source of incorne. other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial J.ending
institution in the regular course of business on
terns available to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating tuo hundred fifty
dollars (5250) or nore in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the tine when the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
enployee, or holds any position of nanagement.
Section 87103 (c) and (d).
Planning Cornrnissioners are public officials 
subject to the
conf lict-of- interest provisions of the Act. 
(Section 82048.)
Therefore, planning co:nmissioners nay not participate in or use
their off icial. position to influence any- governmental decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on their econonic interests.
Presumably, you receive incone of $25o or nore per year fron
your employer. Therefore,. you must disqualify yourself fron any
decision which will foreseeably and naterially affect your
employer, $rhere such effect is distinguishable from the effect on
the public Aenera l Iy .
Foreseeabi 1i tv
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantj.al tikelihood that they wiJ,I olcur. 
To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision nust be nore than a nere


Our FiIe No. I -9O-7 55
Page 4
possibility; 
however, certainty is not required. 
(Downev cares v.
Downev communitv Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal.ApP.3d 983, 898-
991; Witt v. Morrow (19771 7O CaI.App.3d 817, A22; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198 (copy enclosed) . ) The Act seeks to prevent
more than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even
the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v.
lIorro$r, supra, at 823. )
In the circunstances posited by you, it is reasonably
foreseeabLe that there could be a financial effect upon your
enployer where Dewalt is perforning services adjacent to a
development which is subject to a planning comrnission decision.
Many decisions regarding residential housing developnents could
have an irnpact on neighboring developments that are in the
planning stage. Similarly, where DewaIt itself is providing
services on a project subject to planning conmisslon review, it is
foreseeable that there could be a financial impact on DeWaIt
resulting from that review.
Once it has been deterrnined that it is foreseeable that a
decision could have a financial effect on a public official's
source of income, it nust be determined whether the effect will be
material.
Materia l itv
The initial 
step in assessing the materiality of the effect
of a governmental decision requires a determination as to whether
the involvement of the official's 
economic interest is direct or
indirect. 
Regulation L8702.1(b), copy enclosed, states that a
person or business entity is directly involved in a decision
before an official's 
agency when that person or entity, either
personally or by an agent:
(1) fnitiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be nade by filing an application,
cIain, appeal., or sinilar request or;
(2) Is a naned party in, or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official 
or the official.,s agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any J.icense, pernit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702. 1(b) .


Our File No. I-9O-7 55
Page 5
An official's 
economic interest is also considered to be
directly invol-ved in a decision if there is a nexus between the
purpose for which the official 
receives income and the
governmental decision. 
(Regulation la702.1(a) (1) . )
There is a nexus betlreen the purpose for which an official
receives incone and a governnental decision if the official
receives income to achieve a goal or purpose $rhich would be
achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by the governmental
decision. 
(Regulation :-8702.1(d).) If you participate in
planning commission decisions relating to your own engineering
work, you would be using your public position to achieve results
relating to your private work. Under these circumstances, there
would appear to be a nexus between planning commission decisions
and your performance of your private contract. Therefore, you
nust disqualify yourself fron participating in pJ.anning commission
decisions relating to the project. 
(Silver Advice Letter, No' I-
9o-644; Lopus Advice Letter, No. I-90-152; copies enclosed. )
When your econornic interest is directly involved in a
decision, you tnay not participate in the decision unless it will
have !9 financial effect on that economic interest' 
(Regulation
18702.1.) Therefore, you may not participate in planning
comnission decisions which wiII have any financial effect upon the
engineering cotnpany which is a source of income to you.
Decisions may also come before the planning commission which
might indirectly affect your cotnpany. When a decision wiII have
an indirect effect on a business entity which has been a source of
income to a public official, 
the appropriate standard for
deterrnining rnateriality is that of Regulation L8702.2, copy
enclosed. We have not been apprised of the size of DeWalt, and
thus cannot point with particularity to the subdivision of
Regulation L8702-2 that is pertinent to your employer. We suggest
that you review the regulati.on, utilizing the threshold anounts
that apply to DelvaLt, and then deterrnine whether the anticipated
decisions would have effects that r.rould meet the rnateriality test
that would be applicable to a business entity of that size.
For example, for a relatively small co:npany, the test for
materiality found in Regulation fa702.2 (g) j.s as follows:


Our File No.  I-90-755
Page 6
(1) The decision will resul.t in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of S1o,00O or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or elirninating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the anount of 52,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will resul-t in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
Regulation :-87 0?.2 (g\ .
If Dewalt were a company of the appropriate size for
Regulation l^8702.2(g), and if a planning commission decision had
an effect that met any of the threshold amounts listed above, then
you could not participate in that decision, because the effect on
Dewalt would be deemed to be material. 
(see Fitzqerald Advice
Letter, No. I-90-469, coPy enclosed. )
whereas the financial effect of a decision may not aLlrays be
easily assessed, it is incunbent upon you as a public official. who
has a financial interest to make a good faith effort to determine
to the best of your ability the effect of the decision on your
financial interest, which in this case is your employer. (Weedman
Advice Letter No. I-90-759, copy enclosed. )
The above analysis would not be altered If you obtain a
position of management lrith Devlalt, unlessr in addition to a
management position, you are given ownership amounting to 10
percent or more of the conpany. section 82030(a). In that case,
(assurning your ownership interest amounted to at least one
thousand dolLars), the sources of income to the company woul.d be
considered to be sources of incorne to you. (Bovd Advice Letter
No. A-84-281, copy enclosed. ) Honever, if in obtaining a
nanagement position, you remain sirnply a salaried employee with no
ownership interest, then DeWalt, rather than its clients,
constitutes your source of incorne. We suggest that you contact us
again if you are given an ownership interest of ten percent of
nore in Dewalt, so that lre can advise you in connection with
participation in planning comrnission decisions.
You have also asked if our analysis would differ wi.th
respect to projects of the developer empJ.oying your engineering
firn if you do not personally participate in ihose projects. 
As a
general ru1e, our analysis would be the sane. The engineering
firm would still 
constitute an economic interest, beciuse it would
renain a source of incorne to you. Thus, any decision which could
have a :naterial financial effect on your f ii:n would require your
disqualification. 
Additional-Iy, youi interests and those of the
f irrn are linked. This would be even clearer in the event you


Our File No.  
■-90-755
Page 7
were promoted to a position of Danagement in the company.
Typica1ly, an etTrployee is paid to pronote the employer's
interests, and it would be against the enployer's interest to have
any of its projects rejected by the planning cornmission. (silver
Advice Letter, No. I-90-644, copy enclosed.) Therefore, the above
discussion of nexus issues uould be equally applicable here.
I trust the above answers
further information′
 please do
322-590■
.
SH:SLB:dg
Enclosures
your questions.  If you need
not hesitate to call me at (9■
6)
Sincerely′
SCOTT HALLABRIN
chief counsel
